critique.summary: The article demonstrates how to integrate the C-based raylib library with Swift for macOS and WebAssembly, highlighting the ease of using Swift's Clang importer to interact with C code without manual FFI bindings. It also discusses building and running a simple raylib project across platforms, including WebAssembly, with practical tips and considerations.
mainPoints: Swift can directly interact with C libraries like raylib using the Clang importer, avoiding manual FFI bindings.
The project structure involves separating raylib's static libraries and headers from the Swift code, with a module map facilitating the integration.
Building for WebAssembly requires additional steps, including writing C stubs and using emcc, with attention to browser limitations like infinite loops.
The approach simplifies cross-platform game development with raylib and Swift, leveraging Swift Package Manager for dependencies.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the process of integrating C libraries with Swift, including technical details about the Clang importer, module maps, and cross-platform building. These methods are consistent with official Swift documentation and community practices. The claims about Swift's ability to interact seamlessly with C code and the process for building WebAssembly projects are supported by existing tools and techniques, making the overall information reliable.
considerations: The article does not provide detailed instructions for setting up the environment or installing necessary SDKs, which may be challenging for beginners.
Building WebAssembly projects with Swift involves complex steps and potential compatibility issues not fully explored in the article.
Performance implications of using Swift with C libraries and WebAssembly are not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the practicality and challenges of using Swift with Raylib for game development, especially regarding interoperability with C++ libraries and WASM build processes. Participants debate Swift's compile times, ecosystem limitations, and technical aspects of language bindings and performance.

comments.author: enbugger
comments.text:
> So, if you want to build games using raylib, why not learn learning or use Swift for that?

Never ever worked for me. Imagine, you actually learned basic Swift and Raylib, now you want "advanced" features in your game like navigation/pathfinding, model loading, ImGui, skeletal animation (those are actually vital for gamedev). You realize that

- Navigation is only ReCast which is pure C++ library,

- ImGui has C++ API as first-class citizen,

- Decent animation with compression is only open-sourced by OzzAnimation which is pure C++ project.

For gamedev interfacing with C is never enough, half of ecosystem is built heavily on C++. Even C++ interop is not enough (see Dlang). Without all those libraries you are bound to make boring 2d platformers.

Same for Zig, Odin, C3 etc.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and accurate critique of the limitations of using Swift and C interoperability for advanced game development, highlighting the importance of C++ libraries.

comments.author: flohofwoe
comments.text:
Raylib has a C API though, and this is 'easy-mode' in any language (even without integrated bindings generation like Swift or Zig have - e.g. it's fairly trivial to write a bindings generator that uses Clang's ast-dump json file format as input and translates the API declarations into another language.

A more interesting question would be how well the C++ interoperability works that was added in Swift 5.9, does it work with all C++ headers, even headers that make extensive use of template code. Also how does Swift extract information needed for lifetime tracking, e.g. C++ APIs that return smart pointers and object lifetime ends on the caller's side. Does this only work for C++ stdlib smart pointer types, or are custom types also supported.
comments.analysis: This comment intelligently discusses the technical nuances of Swift's interoperability with C and C++ APIs, raising valid questions about template support and lifetime management.

comments.author: keldaris
comments.text: Out of curiosity, how does the size and performance of the generated WASM compare to just compiling the same Raylib example from the equivalent C code via Emscripten? In other words, how much overhead does the choice to use Swift add here or in general?
comments.analysis: This comment constructively requests a performance comparison, which is a relevant and practical inquiry for evaluating Swift's overhead in WASM builds.

comments.author: leecommamichael
comments.text:
> So, if you want to build games using raylib, why not learn learning or use Swift for that?

Because Apple won't fix Swift's abysmal compile times, and there are languages with similar or better ergonomics without that flaw.
comments.analysis: While the comment expresses a common frustration about Swift's compile times, it lacks detailed evidence but reflects a widely acknowledged issue.

comments.author: acarette
comments.text:
Thanks for sharing it!

I might be interested on how to build the WASM build more easier with Swift tools. I guess some tools exist to facilitate the final builds, but did not found it...

Do not hesitate to comment if someone experienced this before.
comments.analysis: This comment is a polite inquiry about tooling for WASM builds with Swift, showing constructive engagement but not providing technical insights.
